threats.title: Critical Fee Sweeping and Claim DoS via `pool.collect(max)`
threats.hypothesis_description: The `claim` function calls `pool.collect` with `type(uint128).max`, sweeping all accumulated fees for the entire shared Uniswap V3 position. It sends the user's calculated share to the user and the remainder to the `FEE_TAKER`. This steals other users' uncollected fees. Subsequent claims will revert due to an underflow when calculating `amountCollected0 - amount0`, permanently bricking the contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a fundamental flaw in how shared Uniswap V3 positions are managed. It guarantees loss of funds and a complete denial of service for all users except the first claimer.

proof_of_signal.pattern: pool.collect with type(uint128).max sweeps all fees, followed by subtraction of user share and transfer of remainder to feeTaker

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Sweeping shared position fees and sending remainder to fee taker
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: amountCollected0 - amount0 will underflow for subsequent claimers
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No tracking of individual user fee balances within the contract

analysis_questions: Does pool.collect(max) actually collect fees belonging to other users?
Will amountCollected0 - amount0 underflow on the second claim?
validation_requirements: Simulate two users claiming sequentially after fees have accumulated
Verify the fee taker receives the second user's fees during the first claim

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: User A claims their vested tokens. The contract sweeps all fees from Uniswap, gives User A their share, and sends the rest to the fee taker. User B attempts to claim. The contract calculates User B's fee share, but the Uniswap pool has no fees left to collect. The contract attempts to subtract User B's share from the collected amount (which is 0), causing an underflow and reverting the transaction.
prerequisites: At least two users with vested positions
Accumulated fees in the Uniswap pool

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of all accumulated trading fees for users; permanent lock of vested liquidity for all subsequent claimers
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users except the first claimer
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None, funds are permanently locked or stolen

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing integration tests with multiple users claiming after fee accumulation

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Any time after launch and fee accumulation
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Easily detectable on-chain via reverted claim transactions

threats.title: Complete Whitelist Bypass due to Default Mapping Value Logic Flaw
threats.hypothesis_description: In `Whitelist.checkWhitelist`, the condition `if (_allowedWhitelistIndex == 0 || _whitelistIndex[to] > _allowedWhitelistIndex)` is intended to block unwhitelisted users. However, unmapped addresses have a default `_whitelistIndex` of 0. If `_allowedWhitelistIndex` is greater than 0, `0 > _allowedWhitelistIndex` evaluates to false, bypassing the revert. This allows any unwhitelisted address to buy tokens freely.
threats.investigation_rationale: This logic flaw completely invalidates the core security mechanism of the whitelist phase, allowing Sybil attacks and unauthorized participation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Logical OR with default mapping value 0 bypasses greater-than check

preconditions: _allowedWhitelistIndex is set to a value greater than 0

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Incorrect handling of default mapping values in access control logic
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Unwhitelisted users bypass the revert condition

analysis_questions: Does the condition evaluate to false for unwhitelisted users?
Can unwhitelisted users successfully execute a buy transaction?
validation_requirements: Deploy the contract, set _allowedWhitelistIndex to 1
Attempt a buy transaction from an unwhitelisted address

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: The owner sets `_allowedWhitelistIndex` to 1 to allow the first batch of whitelisted users to buy. An attacker, who is not whitelisted, initiates a buy transaction. Their `_whitelistIndex` is 0. The condition `0 > 1` is false, so the transaction does not revert. The attacker successfully buys tokens.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Unauthorized users can drain the token supply intended for whitelisted participants
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Legitimate whitelisted users who may be front-run or crowded out
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None, once tokens are bought they cannot be clawed back

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests verifying that unwhitelisted users are blocked when _allowedWhitelistIndex > 0

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible on-chain as buys from unwhitelisted addresses

threats.title: Per-User Cap Bypass via NFT Transfer
threats.hypothesis_description: The `buy` function enforces `maxCapPerUser` by checking `raiseAmount <= saleInfo.maxCapPerUser - _position.raiseAmount`. However, it determines whether to create a new position based on `balanceOf(recipient) == 0`. A user can buy their maximum cap, transfer the received NFT to another address, and then call `buy` again. Since their balance is now 0, a new NFT is minted with a reset `raiseAmount`, allowing them to bypass the cap indefinitely.
threats.investigation_rationale: This breaks the fair distribution model of the ILO, allowing whales to monopolize the token sale.

proof_of_signal.pattern: balanceOf check allows minting new NFT with reset raiseAmount after transfer

preconditions: User has sufficient funds to buy maxCapPerUser multiple times

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: State reset via token transfer bypassing accumulation limits
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: raiseAmount tracking is tied to the NFT, not the user's historical actions
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No mapping tracking total historical contributions per address

analysis_questions: Does transferring the NFT reset balanceOf to 0?
Does a new NFT get minted with a fresh raiseAmount of 0?
validation_requirements: Simulate a user buying max cap, transferring the NFT, and buying again

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A whitelisted user buys `maxCapPerUser` tokens and receives an NFT. They transfer this NFT to a secondary wallet. They call `buy` again from the original wallet. The contract sees `balanceOf == 0`, mints a new NFT, and allows another `maxCapPerUser` purchase.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Monopolization of the ILO by a single well-funded actor
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Other whitelisted users who are crowded out

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for consecutive purchases interleaved with NFT transfers

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible on-chain via multiple mints to the same address

threats.title: Missing Upper Bound Check for Initial Price in `initILOPool`
threats.hypothesis_description: In `ILOManager.initILOPool`, the validation `require(sqrtRatioLowerX96 < _project.initialPoolPriceX96 && sqrtRatioLowerX96 < sqrtRatioUpperX96, "RANGE");` fails to check if `_project.initialPoolPriceX96 < sqrtRatioUpperX96`. If a project admin sets an initial price at or above the upper tick, the liquidity math in `ILOPool` will be broken, resulting in incorrect token amounts being required or deployed.
threats.investigation_rationale: This allows the creation of malformed ILO pools that violate the core economic assumptions of concentrated liquidity, potentially leading to failed launches or loss of sale tokens.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Missing upper bound check for initialPoolPriceX96 against sqrtRatioUpperX96

preconditions: Project admin initializes pool with initialPoolPriceX96 >= sqrtRatioUpperX96

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Allows initial price to be outside the upper bound of the tick range

analysis_questions: How does `_saleAmountNeeded` behave when initial price >= upper tick?
Will `addLiquidity` fail or deploy single-sided liquidity incorrectly?
validation_requirements: Deploy a pool with initial price > upper tick
Simulate buys and launch to observe the resulting Uniswap position

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A project admin maliciously or accidentally sets `tickUpper` such that the initial price is above it. Investors buy positions. At launch, `_saleAmountNeeded` calculates a positive requirement for `SALE_TOKEN` due to internal variable swapping, but `pool.mint` requires 0 `SALE_TOKEN`. The pool launches with 100% `RAISE_TOKEN` and is completely out of range.
prerequisites: Admin access to initialize a project

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Investors receive malformed positions; potential loss of value
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All investors in the malformed pool
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Low, requires manual intervention if funds are stuck

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing edge case tests for initial price outside the tick range

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible on-chain via pool configuration parameters

threats.title: Oracle Manipulation for Newly Created Pools Bypasses ETH Contribution Cap
threats.hypothesis_description: `UniswapV3Oracle.peek` uses `longestPeriod` if it is less than the 30-minute `PERIOD`. For newly created pools, `longestPeriod` can be extremely short (e.g., 1 block). This effectively turns the TWAP into a spot price oracle, which can be easily manipulated via a flash loan or large swap. An attacker can artificially lower the VULT price, causing the oracle to return a tiny ETH estimate, bypassing the `_maxAddressCap` in `Whitelist.checkWhitelist`.
threats.investigation_rationale: Oracle manipulation is a highly common and critical attack vector. The fallback to a short period completely negates the security benefits of a TWAP.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Fallback to shortest observation period for new pools enables spot price manipulation

preconditions: Pool is newly created (longestPeriod is very small)
Attacker has capital to manipulate the spot price

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Unsafe fallback to short TWAP periods
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Allows spot price manipulation to affect oracle readings
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No minimum safe period enforcement (e.g., reverting if longestPeriod < 5 minutes)

analysis_questions: Can the spot price be manipulated enough to significantly alter the ETH estimate?
Can the attacker execute the manipulation and the buy in the same or adjacent blocks?
validation_requirements: Simulate a flash loan or large swap to manipulate the pool price
Verify that the oracle returns the manipulated price and allows a massive buy

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Immediately after the Uniswap pool is created, an attacker executes a massive swap to crash the VULT price. The oracle, using a 1-block period, returns this crashed price. The attacker then buys a massive amount of VULT through the whitelist contract, as the estimated ETH value is artificially low, bypassing the 3 ETH cap. They then reverse the swap.
prerequisites: Capital for large swap or flash loan access

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Attacker monopolizes the token supply, bypassing fair distribution caps
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Other participants who are crowded out

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for oracle behavior with newly created pools under price manipulation

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: High (requires significant capital to move the Uniswap pool price)
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible on-chain via large swaps sandwiching a buy transaction

threats.title: Griefing Attack via Third-Party Purchases in `buy`
threats.hypothesis_description: The `buy` function in `ILOPool` allows the `msg.sender` to specify a different `recipient`. The whitelist and cap checks are performed against the `recipient`, but the `msg.sender` pays the `raiseAmount`. An attacker can maliciously fill the `maxCapPerUser` allocation of a whitelisted user by paying for them, preventing the victim from participating in the ILO with their own funds.
threats.investigation_rationale: While it costs the attacker money, griefing attacks are highly realistic in competitive token launches where excluding a competitor is valuable.

proof_of_signal.pattern: msg.sender pays but recipient state is updated, allowing forced cap consumption

preconditions: Attacker knows the address of a whitelisted competitor
Attacker is willing to spend funds to grief the competitor

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Allows consuming another user's allocation without their consent
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing `require(msg.sender == recipient)` or explicit delegation mechanism

analysis_questions: Can an attacker successfully call buy for another recipient?
Does this prevent the recipient from buying later?
validation_requirements: Simulate an attacker calling buy with a victim's address as recipient
Verify the victim cannot subsequently buy

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: An attacker identifies a key competitor's whitelisted address. The attacker calls `buy(maxCapPerUser, competitorAddress)`. The attacker's funds are spent, and the competitor receives the NFT. When the competitor tries to participate with their own funds, the transaction reverts because their cap is exhausted.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Competitors are blocked from participating; attacker loses funds but achieves strategic goal
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None, the cap is permanently consumed

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for third-party purchases and their impact on user caps

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible on-chain as buys where msg.sender != recipient

threats.title: Division by Zero in `_unlockedLiquidity` Bricks Claims
threats.hypothesis_description: In `ILOPool._unlockedLiquidity`, if a vesting schedule is configured with `start == end`, the calculation `(vest.end - vest.start) * BPS` results in zero. This causes `FullMath.mulDiv` to revert with a division by zero error, permanently bricking claims for any user assigned to that vesting schedule.
threats.investigation_rationale: Configuration errors or malicious admin actions during initialization can lead to permanently locked funds due to this missing validation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Division by (vest.end - vest.start) without ensuring start < end

preconditions: Project admin configures a vesting schedule with start == end

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Unsafe division by a potentially zero value
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Reverts all claims for the affected schedule

analysis_questions: Does `_validateVestSchedule` allow `start == end`?
Does `_unlockedLiquidity` revert when `start == end`?
validation_requirements: Initialize a pool with a zero-duration vest
Attempt to claim and verify the division by zero revert

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A project admin accidentally or maliciously initializes a pool with a vesting schedule where `start == end` (e.g., intending an immediate unlock). Investors buy into the pool. After launch, when investors attempt to claim, the transaction reverts due to division by zero, permanently locking their funds.
prerequisites: Admin access to initialize a pool

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent lock of all vested liquidity for the affected schedule
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All investors assigned to the malformed schedule

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for zero-duration vesting schedules

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible on-chain via pool configuration parameters

threats.title: Fee-on-Transfer Tokens Break Refund Accounting
threats.hypothesis_description: If `RAISE_TOKEN` is a fee-on-transfer token, the `buy` function records the full `raiseAmount` in `_positions[tokenId].raiseAmount` but the contract receives less. During a refund, the contract attempts to return the full `raiseAmount` to users. The last users to claim refunds will find the contract has insufficient balance, permanently losing their funds.
threats.investigation_rationale: The protocol does not explicitly forbid fee-on-transfer tokens, and standard `transferFrom` usage without balance checks is a known vulnerability pattern for such tokens.
deployment_assumptions: RAISE_TOKEN is configured as a fee-on-transfer token
threats.config_evidence: No deployment evidence provided regarding the specific RAISE_TOKEN to be used. The code assumes standard ERC20 behavior.

proof_of_signal.pattern: TransferHelper.safeTransferFrom used without checking actual received balance

preconditions: Pool fails to launch, triggering refunds

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Assuming transferFrom amount equals received amount
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing pre/post balance check during deposit

analysis_questions: Does the protocol intend to support fee-on-transfer tokens?
Will the refund process fail for the last users?
validation_requirements: Deploy a pool with a mock fee-on-transfer token
Simulate buys and a subsequent refund phase
Verify the last refund reverts due to insufficient balance

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A project uses a token with a 5% transfer fee as the `RAISE_TOKEN`. Users deposit 100 tokens, but the contract only receives 95. The contract records 100 in `raiseAmount`. The pool fails to launch. Users claim refunds. The first users get their full 100 back, draining the contract faster than expected. The last 5% of users cannot claim their refunds because the contract is empty.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of funds for the last users to claim refunds

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests with non-standard ERC20 tokens (fee-on-transfer)

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible on-chain via reverted refund transactions

threats.title: `setRefundDeadlineForProject` Can Permanently Lock Funds
threats.hypothesis_description: The `ILOManager` owner can call `setRefundDeadlineForProject` to change the refund deadline to any value, including `type(uint64).max`. If a pool fails to launch (e.g., due to soft cap not met or price manipulation), and the owner maliciously or accidentally sets the deadline to max, investors will never be able to claim refunds, permanently locking their `RAISE_TOKEN` deposits.
threats.investigation_rationale: Unbounded admin privileges over critical lifecycle timestamps can lead to permanent loss of user funds, violating trust assumptions.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Unbounded setter for refundDeadline allows permanent lock of funds

initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing upper bound check (e.g., `require(refundDeadline <= launchTime + MAX_OFFSET)`)

analysis_questions: Can the owner set the deadline to an arbitrarily high value?
Does this permanently block the `refundable` modifier?
validation_requirements: Simulate the owner setting the deadline to `type(uint64).max`
Verify that `claimRefund` reverts indefinitely

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A pool fails to reach its soft cap. Before the refund deadline is reached, a compromised `ILOManager` owner calls `setRefundDeadlineForProject` and sets the deadline to the year 3000. Investors attempt to call `claimRefund`, but the transaction reverts because the deadline has not been reached. Their funds are locked forever.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Total loss of all deposited funds in the affected pool
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Only if the owner resets the deadline to a valid time

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for extreme values in `setRefundDeadlineForProject`

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Any time before refund is triggered
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible on-chain via the setter transaction

threats.title: Unbounded Fee Configuration Causes Underflow DoS
threats.hypothesis_description: The `ILOManager` owner can set `PLATFORM_FEE` and `PERFORMANCE_FEE` to values greater than 10000 (100%). If this occurs, the `_deductFees` function in `ILOPool` will attempt to subtract an amount larger than the input amount, causing an integer underflow (in Solidity 0.7.6) and reverting all user claims.
threats.investigation_rationale: Missing bounds checks on fee setters can lead to accidental or malicious denial of service for core protocol functions.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Unbounded fee setters allow values > 10000, causing underflow in _deductFees

preconditions: A new project is initialized capturing this fee

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Missing upper bound validation on fee parameters
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Allows fees > 100%, causing underflow during deduction

analysis_questions: Can the owner set fees > 10000?
Does `_deductFees` underflow when fees > 10000?
validation_requirements: Set platform fee to 20000
Initialize a project and launch it
Attempt to claim and verify the underflow revert

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: The `ILOManager` owner accidentally sets the `PLATFORM_FEE` to 20000 instead of 2000. A new project is initialized, caching this fee. After launch, investors attempt to claim their liquidity. `_deductFees` calculates the fee as 200% of the amount, and the subtraction `amount0 - fee` underflows, reverting the transaction. All claims for this project are permanently bricked.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent lock of all vested liquidity for projects initialized with the bad fee
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All investors in the affected projects
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None, as fees are cached per project at initialization

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible on-chain via the setter transaction
